BOUGAINVILLEA.description: A vigorous climbing shrub whose vivid 'petals' are actually bracts — modified leaves — not true flowers. The real flowers are tiny and white, hidden at the centre. Extremely popular across tropical India and South Asia.

CHRYSANTHEMUM.description: A layered, multi-petaled flower closely tied to autumn and widely grown for temple offerings and garden display across East and South Asia.

COSMOS.description: A delicate daisy-like flower with feathery foliage. Easy to grow from seed and popular with butterflies and bees; self-seeds freely once established.

GIANT CALOTROPE.description: A large shrub with thick, waxy leaves and star-shaped flowers important in Hindu worship (offered to Lord Shiva). The plant produces a milky latex used in traditional medicine across South Asia.

GLEBIONIS CORONARIA.description: Also called Shungiku in Japanese and Tong Ho in Chinese, its young leaves are cooked in stir-fries and hotpots. The bright daisy-like flowers are also grown ornamentally.

HIBISCUS.description: Large trumpet-shaped flowers that open for a single day. The national flower of Malaysia and deeply embedded in Indian Ayurvedic hair and skin care.

MARIGOLD.description: The most widely used flower in Indian festivals and weddings. Planted in vegetable gardens to deter pests; the strong scent repels insects naturally.

NAYANTARA.description: Called Nayantara ('star of the eye') in Bengali, this common roadside flower is medically significant — it is the source of vincristine and vinblastine, chemotherapy drugs used to treat leukaemia and other cancers.

PETUNIA.description: A popular garden annual with funnel-shaped flowers that come in almost every colour. Trailing varieties are particularly favoured for window boxes and hanging baskets.

TAGAR.description: Known as Chandni in Hindi and Tagar in Bengali, the pinwheel-shaped white flowers are offered in Hindu worship. Very commonly found in West Bengal and throughout South Asia.
